questions.question: What does the 2025 systematic review say about the evidence quality for green exercise (outdoor movement in nature)?

options.text: Green exercise has STRONG, definitive evidence as a biological magic bullet
The evidence is MIXED/EMERGING — 15 RCTs showed promising effects, but evidence quality was limited and results heterogeneous; it's not a silver bullet
Outdoor exercise has been proven harmful to mental health
There is no research on green exercise at all

questions.explanation: A 2025 systematic review found promising mental health effects from 15 RCTs of urban green exercise, but evidence quality was limited and results heterogeneous (some positive, some null). The lesson emphasizes green exercise is NOT a biological magic bullet — the primary benefit appears to be motivational (adherence) rather than fundamentally different neurobiological mechanisms. It's a valuable tool, but not a replacement for evidence-based exercise protocols.

questions.question: What is the 'reduced perceived exertion' effect of outdoor exercise, and why does it matter?

options.text: Outdoor exercise doesn't affect how hard exercise feels
At the same heart rate and intensity, outdoor exercise feels EASIER than indoor — making it more enjoyable and sustainable, which improves adherence (the most critical variable)
Outdoor exercise always feels harder than indoor
Perceived exertion has no impact on exercise adherence

questions.explanation: Research shows that at equivalent heart rate/intensity, people report outdoor exercise feels less hard than indoor. Why: distraction from physical discomfort (nature provides visual/auditory stimuli vs. staring at a timer), novelty (changing scenery vs. repetitive indoor), and positive affect (nature improves mood). This matters because if exercise feels easier and more enjoyable, you're more likely to sustain it — and adherence is the most critical variable for mental health benefits.

questions.question: According to Attention Restoration Theory, how does nature help mental fatigue?

options.text: Nature has no effect on mental fatigue
Natural environments engage involuntary attention (effortless — birds, trees, flowing water) allowing directed attention (effortful focus from work/study) to rest and recover
Nature increases mental fatigue
Only medication can relieve mental fatigue

questions.explanation: Attention Restoration Theory posits two attention systems: directed attention (effortful focus — work, studying) fatigues quickly, and involuntary attention (effortless engagement with beauty, nature, novelty) restores capacity. Nature engages involuntary attention (watching leaves move, listening to birds) without requiring effort, allowing directed attention to recover. This makes outdoor exercise feel more restorative than gym-based movement for mentally exhausted people.

questions.question: When might indoor exercise be preferable to outdoor exercise?

options.text: Indoor exercise is never preferable; outdoor is always better
Indoor is preferable for: weather extremes, safety concerns, time constraints, specific equipment needs (weights, machines), social anxiety outdoors, or accessibility (wheelchair-friendly facilities)
Only lazy people exercise indoors
Outdoor exercise is required for any mental health benefits

questions.explanation: The lesson emphasizes: the 'best' environment is the one that maximizes adherence. Indoor is better when: extreme heat/cold/air quality issues, unsafe neighborhoods or no green space access, gym is faster than driving to trailhead, you need specific equipment (strength training), you have social anxiety outdoors, or you need accessibility features (wheelchair access, climate control). Neurobiological benefits are equivalent — choose based on what you'll actually sustain.

questions.question: Based on the lesson's assessment question: if you have NO meaningful access to green spaces, what should you do?

options.text: Give up on exercise entirely since outdoor is the only effective option
Focus on indoor movement OR explore urban parks/greenways if available — the movement itself is what matters most; lack of 'nature' shouldn't stop you
Move to the countryside immediately
Only outdoor exercise provides mental health benefits

questions.explanation: The lesson is clear: if you have limited or no nature access, focus on indoor movement. The neurobiological mechanisms (BDNF, monoamines, HPA axis, endocannabinoids) work the same indoors as outdoors. Urban parks or small green spaces can provide some benefit, but don't let lack of forests/mountains prevent you from moving. Adherence to ANY consistent movement matters far more than the setting.
